seo.title: Building Marketplaces with open-source tools
seo.description: A step-by-step implementation guide for Marketplaces with open-source tools.

content.intro: This guide outlines the technical implementation of a multi-party payment system for a two-sided marketplace using Stripe Connect. We focus on the 'Destination Charges' model, which allows a platform to facilitate transactions between buyers and sellers while automatically deducting a commission fee before funds reach the seller's account.
prerequisites: Node.js environment with the 'stripe' npm package installed
Database (e.g., PostgreSQL) with a schema supporting User and Listing entities
A secure endpoint for receiving HTTPS webhooks

steps.title: Extend User Schema for Connect Account Mapping
steps.explanation: To facilitate payments, you must map your application's 'Seller' entity to a Stripe Connect Account ID. Update your database schema to store the Stripe Account ID and a status flag indicating if the seller has completed the onboarding process.
pitfalls: Storing the Stripe Secret Key in the database instead of environment variables.
Failing to index the stripe_connect_id column, leading to slow lookups during webhook processing.

steps.title: Implement the Onboarding Link Flow
steps.explanation: Create an Express or Next.js API route that generates a Stripe Account Link. This link redirects the seller to Stripe's hosted onboarding UI. Upon completion, Stripe redirects the user back to your platform.
pitfalls: Assuming a single redirect means onboarding is finished; you must verify the 'details_submitted' state via webhooks or account retrieval.
Hardcoding return URLs instead of using environment-specific base URLs.

steps.title: Create Checkout Sessions with Application Fees
steps.explanation: When a buyer initiates a purchase, create a Stripe Checkout Session. Use the 'transfer_data' parameter to specify the seller's account and the 'application_fee_amount' to define your platform's commission in cents.
pitfalls: Calculating fees on the client-side, which allows users to manipulate the commission amount.
Neglecting to handle currency precision; Stripe requires amounts in the smallest currency unit (e.g., cents).

steps.title: Handle Webhook Events for State Reconciliation
steps.explanation: Listen for 'checkout.session.completed' and 'account.updated' events. Use these to update your internal database records for order fulfillment and seller verification status.
pitfalls: Processing webhooks without verifying the 'stripe-signature' header, exposing your DB to spoofed requests.
Not returning a 200 status code immediately, causing Stripe to retry and potentially double-process events.

steps.title: Implement Refund Logic with Fee Reversal
steps.explanation: When a buyer requests a refund, you must decide if the platform fee is also refunded. Use the 'reverse_transfer' and 'refund_application_fee' flags to manage the flow of funds back from the seller's Connect account.
pitfalls: Attempting to refund more than the seller's current Stripe balance, which results in a failed refund and requires manual intervention.
Forgetting to reverse the transfer, which leaves the seller with the money while the platform absorbs the refund cost.

content.conclusion: By implementing this flow, you have established a scalable payment architecture that handles automated splitting and seller onboarding. Ensure you monitor your Stripe Dashboard for 'Requirements Due' flags on Connect accounts to prevent payout blocks as regulations evolve.
